notes: The doctrinally load-bearing terms are dehī (the embodied dweller), nityam avadhyaḥ (eternally inviolable), sarvasya dehe (in the body of all), and the universalising tasmāt sarvāṇi bhūtāni (therefore for all beings). The engineering layer preserves all four: the customer-need is rendered as 'inviolable in every implementation that has ever served it'; the universal scope is preserved as 'do not grieve any particular system's death'; the 'tasmāt' is named explicitly as 'the whole point of this verse.' Shankara's partlessness/all-pervasiveness reading and Ramanuja's universal-scope reading (gods to immovables) are both honestly summarised in the source pack and not conflated. The bridge function (closing the imperishable section, opening to the duty argument at 2.31) is preserved through the closing 'After this verse, the chapter pivots to duty.' PASS.
